content:
Last month, I sat through a strategy presentation. I could tell the slides were from Claude. All the signs were there — the color blocks on the slides (the color differences serving no purpose whatsoever), the too-small font, the cluttered flowcharts. But it wasn’t a big deal. At this point, I’d be surprised if they didn’t use AI.
Then I noticed something on the slide that didn’t make sense, so I asked the presenter why they’d chosen it. Maybe it was an angle I hadn’t thought about.
He looked at the slide for a few seconds before asking me to repeat the question. I did.
“Yeah,” he said eventually. “You’re right. It’s not really an appropriate example. I’ll change it.”
The meeting moved on. I didn’t.
Inaccurate slides were not the issue. I’ve sat through worse presentations with decks created by humans. It was because no one in the room — not even the presenter — seemed to have wrestled with the recommendation before putting it in front of an audience.
I work in a non-technical strategy role at one of the world’s largest consulting firms, a…
